title: How do I get into AI-Trading?
author: SnooHedgehogs3237
selftext:
No I am not looking to buy something from someone, or join a discord server, or etc. etc. 

I’m seriously looking to set up my own AI trading but have no idea where to start. Do I link Claude and teach it my own trading strategy?

Do I give another AI a ton of data and let it figure out a good strategy itself based on pattern recognition?

What AI is best to use for trading?

If someone is seriously able to help me I would really appreciate it. If you guys want to keep a closes circle I also fully understand.

comments.author: AttitudeGrouchy33
comments.body:
I’d start smaller than “teach Claude my strategy” or “give an AI a pile of data and let it find edge.”

Those are usually where people get hurt because the model sounds confident before the system is actually safe.

A better first setup:

1. use AI for research/summarizing, not execution
2. write your trade thesis in a fixed format every time
3. paper trade the ideas
4. log what the AI believed, what would invalidate the trade, sizing, and outcome
5. only then think about broker/API automation

The important split is: the AI can help reason, but it should not be the source of truth for cash, positions, exposure, or risk limits.

If you eventually automate, keep a boring deterministic layer that can say “no” to the model. That’s the part most beginner AI trading setups skip.

comments.author: Bryanadamz
comments.body: Here something I've found useful for my bots, make the bot print out a file in Excel that logs every trade, for backtest and forward testing, record a bunch of market conditions, session times and indicators your bot uses. Then give claude access to the file and find patterns in when the trades are most likely to be profitable, then build a probability engine directly into the bot, bot only takes trades with high probability of success

comments.author: The_AI_Trader
comments.body:
Look into trading agents. We built a platform for this and we found a couple of things that may help you in your quest:

1. The AI needs realtime prices, realtime indicator data. If you just feed it a couple of charts, and let her browse the internet, the results will be very inconsistent. She needs to have access to 5-10 hours of candle data, of different timeframes, different indicators to be able to give proper technical analysis output. If you don't take that route, it will be more like a casino, than an actual analysis, because the sources of information will be dependent on her ability to browse the internet for data, and I can assure you , that data, will be incomplete. She needs 5, 15, 60min, 4hr time frames on differnet stuff like EMAs, RSI, MACD, FIBs, VWAPs, etc. 

2. AI needs another agent doing reserach on the Macro side of things, for the instrument in question. This information is crucial for her to understand direction of trade, and can help you when to decide to chase a particular trend or not. We found that the more aligned with the Macro agent, the trade setup is, the more momentum it can take , and helps us maximize R (risk / benefit) . 

3. AI will give you trade setups, etc. But you also need a final layer that takes that analysis, takes that setup, and decides if it's time to enter or not. This layer is probably one of the most important ones. And this layer takes in a smaller version of all the data, info from macro agent (and a trend agent) to decide if it's time to enter or not. This layer, in our data, showed how the AI rejected 95% of the setups, showing how patient the AI can be. If you ask any professional succesful trader, they will tell you the importance of patience in trading. 

Hope this helps!

replies.author: baconforbabies30
replies.body: Can you share the agent you created? What types of trades does it do? Crypto stock etc? And what trade platform do you use?

replies.author: The_AI_Trader

Check it out. It’s the new world: AI Trading Agents 

Screw staring at charts all day. Time to manage agents and putting ourselves in the fund manager seat, managing risk and deciding who trades and who doesn’t.

comments.author: Either_Door_5500
comments.body:
When you are trying to get an AI model to figure out a trading strategy, the biggest bottleneck is always the data pipeline. Giving a model a raw dump of unformatted text or unstructured numbers usually leads to hallucinations or useless patterns because financial data is incredibly noisy.

If you want to start building an AI trading system, focus heavily on data engineering first. You need structured, clean financial metrics and corporate action histories that an LLM can easily parse without getting confused by missing context or complex adjustments.

I have been working on an API in this space that provides structured and LLM friendly SEC data for devs, which can save you a lot of time on the ingestion layer. It is specifically designed to provide audit trails and has all data needed for proper backtesting without lookahead bias and survivorship bias.  
Happy to share more if you want to take a look.

comments.author: StevenVinyl
comments.body: why would you start from scratch when there's apps like Cod3x out there lol, you're literally going to make life harder for yourself.

comments.author: elliotthamilton
comments.body: That's an interesting concept you brought up. Giving ai some model parameters or tools to create an Ai driven model that's what I'll be launching in a year or less! But I strongly strongly recommend reading perry kaufmans trading systems and methods 6th edition I'm on pag 463 at the bottom Elliott Hamilton its the global standard on intermediate to advanced indicators and techniques that drive the market. And then pick a broker, make a model simulator get a model hook it up to your brokwr.. using claude.

comments.author: rajeshThevar
comments.body:
Nothing is closed knowledge now. You can precisely ask this same question to Claude, GPT 5.5 or run deep research off Gemini, it'll give pointers to start and also explicitly reasonable on its own limitations or limitations it has with what data you have made available for the models. 

Give it a go and let me know. Its the 1% research effort that is going to split from the rest.

comments.author: dom_49_dragon
comments.body: yes ask an AI, also a good step is to check for github repos. there are many free bots available. You should learn a little bit about github then and how to use a repo :)

comments.author: hemepincrawler
comments.body: What i did and what it came out was this: Chat with AI and REFINE an already working strategy, define its rules as best as you can, then engineeer a prompt with said AI, and use the better one for coding, I used Gemini for chat, then Claude for coding, triangulate with a thir AI like Kimi, and find the sweet spot. Test thurally on demo, discipline is your best friend here.

replies.author: StevenVinyl
replies.body: there's a platform that combines all of these called Cod3x btw, even execution.

comments.author: 310to608
comments.body:
Are you currently using an AI tool at all?  Gemini, GPT, etc.?

Here is what I did...as someone who has ZERO financial background and zero tech background;

I registered for Gemini as part of my Google plan.  Then I started using it as a super-up search tool, which is basically the most rudimentary way to use it.  Break out of your established search habits by shifting to more conversational phrasing and follow-up questions to get a sense of how the LLM engages.  Ask it questions about how it works.  Learn the ways it answers and how to phrase questions that prevent hallucinations/fabrications.  Insist that answers must be based on information from reliable (not reddit) sources that can be cited (with functional links), unless you specify you are open to a wider data set. You have to be very specific and direct with things, and making assumptions about persistent memory without instituting failssifs is a fast way to getting bad info.  Do NOT input your personal financial information unless you are running a local LLM (and even then...).  Start asking questions about investment strategies that are specific to your budget/goals, always making sure to contextualize those asks.  Then ask it to run models using historical data.  Run the models against bull, bear and flat markets.  Compare the model against "buy and hold".  Ask for detailed analysis of market trends and assets that offer particularly strong attributes for those models. Be clear about your personal goals and limits.  Then, once you identify an asset and a strategy, suggest a trade and ask if there are ways to improve it. Once you have made a trade, ask it for alert parameters to employ.  Then daily or weekly, ask it to run a detailed market check and suggest potential actions to improve or protect your position.  Challenge it to support the result.  Cross check the numbers it provides against the option chains you get from the brokerage. 

Once you have a proof-of-concept that you are confident in (and recognize the inherent limitations), the next step is to figure out the webhook integration that will allow SOME level of automation once your account qualifies for that use-case based on your brokerage rules. 

But in order to make that shift, you have to rebuild.  I won't use Gemini or GPT for automation, because for security reasons, I am only comfortable using an agent that is fully local (not cloud based or sharing my data).  My plan is to create a walled-garden for an agent that does NOT have access to my email, main accounts, etc., and run a test model on a new portfolio with funds that are completely disposable. It will run on a dedicated machine, via python.  I will have full access/override, and it will not have direct access to my admin-level credentials.

Once I feel the kinks have been identified and addressed to my satisfaction, I will open it up to larger, more hands-off access, but will always require approvals for any actions over a certain threshold to limit exposure.

I think if it as an assistant to hire...but as with any assistant, the efficacy will correlate directly with the level of training and then checks and balances that go into set up and maintenance.

It's a very slow, hands-on process, but it has worked well for me so far.  I started with 8K in November and have built that to a portfolio of just over 17K today.  It hasn't been all-gains, and I made mistakes as I learned the mechanics of various strategies.  Nothing is automated. Yet. Even with the coming changes for PDT limits, most brokerages require an account minimum balance for day trading, and as far as I can tell, the main benefit of automation is the ability to actively manage assets more frequently.  But unless you understand the appropriate guard-rails  (and their impact on a strategy), the theoretical gains that higher turnover suggests is as likely to deliver unintentional losses.

The thing is, there is no "plug and play" solution that doesn't have so many fail-safes to render it wildly inefficient and the mechanics of these systems make them prone to error (by using cached data, hyoer-literal interpretation of nuances data, degrading/corruption of context, etc.)

replies.author: StevenVinyl
replies.body: you should try Cod3x brother, agents trade on hyperliquid

comments.author: tohams
comments.body: Don't. If it were that easy, everybody would do it. Here's why I say that. I trade 3 completely automated strategies. If I ask an LLM/AI anything strategic about them, it provides a straight up AI slop answer. And as someone who knows my strategies well, I'm 100% certain it's just hallucinating. I do use AI to do quantitative analysis of my data but that's historical number crunching, nothing remotely close to strategic. I do use an LLM/AI to create deterministic code that 1) I can verify and 2) gets the same results every time I run it.

comments.author: demon-of-atzoatl91
comments.body: Draconic.ai - acts as a decision support system for humans with all markets with realtime market and institutional grade metrics

comments.author: SuchNeck835
comments.body:
You really need to ask an AI and explain what you want. You can't just 'use AI for trading'. You also can't just shove your data into a ChatGPT window and tell it to 'analyze'. What you can do is let it build a tool for you that either analyses your trades, e.g. Via machine learning, or even better, since you seem to already have your strategy. Just explain your strategy and then tell the AI what you want. 


Id start with a back tester of your strategy so you first prove it's profitable, maybe you can even spot mistakes and make it better. Then you let it either trade or give you signals so you can discretionarily trade them. 


Obviously be sure to test a lot before you let s tool auto trade for you. Start very small so potential mistakes in design don't cost you money.

comments.author: GerManic69
comments.body:
This is the only way...  
Start with a local model such as Llama, you're going to scrape arxiv for ever quantitative trading research paper, algorithmic trading, AI trading, high quality research papers etc...setup a Entei Graph to turn the 500m-1b tokens worth of data into a much deeper training set, do CPT utilizing the graph + research papers on a rented GPU, continue past the point of training loss until the model groks on quantitative/algorithmic trading, setup an agent which interacts with your exchange/broker api, give it money, set it loose. 

The reality is that no model right now is specialized enough to understand quantitative trading on a deeper level, if you dont want to scam yourself, avoid it, or learn ML engineering and specialize an opensource model (no gurantee you can reach grokking) and do it yourself, even then still a ton of risk. ML and trading do not mix as of this point in time, one arxiv paper which analyzed over 800,000 machine learning trading strategies found that less than 10 models actually produced a verifiable quantitative edge.
